ontology.foodex2_description: Halloumi (semi-hard brined cheese from sheep/goat/cow milk, grilled preparation)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for grilled halloumi cheese as a distinct food concept; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled halloumi cheese is approximately 80 g (about 3–4 slices), providing around 246 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 18.4 g fat, and 7.1 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Halloumi Cheese — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Grilled Halloumi Cheese (cr_id G00010353). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record. Saturated fat estimated at 14.0 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for halloumi (NDB 01252), consistent with the HPB record's total fat of 23.06 g/100g and the known fatty acid profile of halloumi (approximately 60–65% saturated fat of total fat). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat values set to -1 as not reported in the HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dairy and high-protein foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for full-fat dairy and semi-hard cheese products
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for halloumi cheese. For high-fat, high-protein semi-hard cheeses, GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low category) based on analogous full-fat dairy entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., full-fat yoghurt GI ~36, full-fat milk GI ~27–34). The high protein (16.09 g/100g) and fat (23.06 g/100g) content substantially blunts any glycaemic response from the 8.85 g/100g carbohydrate (predominantly lactose). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 7.08 g) / 100 = 2.8 (very low). This analogous estimate is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Grilled halloumi cheese is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very high sodium content of 1279 mg/100g; if consumed, limit to a maximum 30 g portion and soak the cheese in fresh water for 30–60 minutes before grilling to reduce sodium by up to 30%, and avoid all additional salt or salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g) with high protein and fat slowing gastric emptying; high caloric density and saturated fat require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled halloumi cheese produces a low glycaemic response (estimated GI ~40) due to its high protein (16.09 g/100g) and fat (23.06 g/100g) content slowing gastric emptying, but its high caloric density (307 kcal/100g) and estimated saturated fat (~14 g/100g) require strict portion control to approximately 60–80 g per serving for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, and grilling is preferred over frying to avoid additional fat absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~14 g/100g) and very high sodium (1279 mg/100g) both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled halloumi cheese carries a high cardiovascular concern due to its estimated saturated fat content of approximately 14 g/100g (exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold) and sodium of 1279 mg/100g, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure respectively; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions (≤30 g) and prioritise lower-sodium, lower-saturated-fat dairy alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~14 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; sodium 1279 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled halloumi cheese is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because its high saturated fat content (~14 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6) and its very high sodium (1279 mg/100g) exacerbates tissue oedema; soaking before grilling and limiting to a 30 g portion may partially reduce sodium load, while the high calcium content (1375 mg/100g) offers some benefit for bone protection in patients on corticosteroids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Halloumi Cheese is a semi-hard, brined cheese originating from Cyprus and widely consumed across the Middle East and Mediterranean region. Halloumi is traditionally made from a blend of sheep, goat, and cow milk, preserved in brine, and is distinctive for its high melting point, which allows it to be grilled or pan-fried without losing its shape. When grilled, the exterior develops a golden-brown crust with characteristic grill marks while the interior remains firm, chewy, and slightly squeaky in texture. The flavour is rich, salty, and mildly tangy, with a savoury depth that intensifies with grilling. Per 100 g, grilled halloumi provides 307 kcal, 16.09 g protein, 23.06 g fat (estimated ~14 g saturated), 8.85 g carbohydrate, 6.24 g sugar, and 1279 mg sodium. It is exceptionally high in calcium (1375 mg/100g), making it nutritionally notable for bone health. Clinically, its very high sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, and its high saturated fat load raises concerns for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the high protein and fat content produces a low glycaemic response (estimated GI ~40), but caloric density and saturated fat require portion control. Grilled halloumi is commonly served as a starter, side dish, or protein component in salads, wraps, and mezze platters. It is gluten-free and suitable for vegetarians. In Singapore, it appears on restaurant menus as a Western or Middle Eastern option.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in halloumi cheese?
Can I eat halloumi if I have high blood pressure?
Is halloumi good for bone health?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010353, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated at 14.0 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy halloumi entry, consistent with the known fatty acid profile of halloumi and the HPB total fat value. GI estimated at 40 (low) from analogous University of Sydney GI Database dairy entries, documented in gi_source. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (14.0 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for halloumi cheese, as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown. This estimate is consistent with halloumi's known composition (~60–65% of total fat as saturated fat). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these fields without risk of misattribution. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; halloumi is a dairy product containing no plant fibre. 4. glycemic_index set to 40 (low category) as an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database full-fat dairy entries; the University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for halloumi. GL per serving = (40 × 7.08 g carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 2.8. 5. food_group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives / Beef' to 'Dairy and eggs / Cheese — semi-hard brined, grilled' as halloumi is a dairy cheese product, not a beef product. 6. The input alias '26. Grilled Halloumi Cheese' contained a menu-numbering prefix; the canonical alias 'Grilled Halloumi Cheese' is used in the output aliases array. 7. This food is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as this is a Middle Eastern/Mediterranean dish with no mandatory local-language requirement under the Veritas multilingual matrix.
